title: 4x4 Truss Bench
summary: A rustic bench with trestle-look 4x4 legs and 2x4 side aprons that read as solid 4x4 from the outside, topped with a breadboard-ended 2x8 top — genuinely chunky, not just wide boards on skinny legs.
description:
Each leg end is its own small truss assembly built from angled 4x4 pieces — there are many ways to join thick 4x4 stock, and pocket holes hidden underneath are one of the cleanest, keeping every fastener out of sight on a piece meant to look solid from every angle. Build two identical leg ends, then attach the bottom stretcher between them.

**The side aprons are 2x4s positioned to read as full 4x4s from the outside** — the trick that makes this bench look far chunkier and heavier than its actual lumber cost. Drill 1-1/2" pocket holes facing upward on the inside of the aprons before attaching them, since those holes are what the benchtop screws into in the next step — drilling them after the aprons are already installed is much harder to reach.

Build the breadboard-ended top as its own assembly first, with 1-1/2" pocket holes and 2-1/2" pocket hole screws, before attaching it to the base — building the top flat on a bench is far easier than fitting boards to an already-standing frame.

materials.name: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

cutList.note: Long point to long point, both ends cut at 10° off square, parallel.
Long point to long point, both ends cut at 10° off square, not parallel.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut two leg top pieces at 13-1/2", four leg diagonal pieces at 13-1/4", two leg feet at 8" and one bottom stretcher at 65" from the 4x4. Cut two side aprons at 65" from the 2x4, 6 ft or 12 ft. Cut two bench top boards at 68-1/2" from the 2x8, 6 ft or 12 ft. Cut two breadboard ends at 14-1/2" from the 2x10, 3 ft (breadboard ends). Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to two 8 ft 4x4, two 6 ft 2x4, 6 ft or 12 ft, two 6 ft 2x8, 6 ft or 12 ft and one 3 ft 2x10, 3 ft (breadboard ends). Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw — several parts here are within half an inch of each other and are easy to mix up later.

steps.title: Build the leg ends
steps.body: Build two leg ends from the leg top and diagonal pieces, using a Kreg Jig to hide the joints underneath if desired.

steps.title: Attach the bottom stretcher
steps.body: Attach the bottom stretcher between the two leg ends.

steps.title: Add the side aprons
steps.body: Add the side aprons, drilling 1-1/2" pocket holes facing upward on the inside before attaching, for use in the next step.

steps.title: Build and attach the top
steps.body: Build the bench top with the breadboard ends using 1-1/2" pocket holes and 2-1/2" pocket hole screws, then attach to the base.

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Sand all surfaces and apply primer and paint, or stain and poly.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly
